course_info.title: Senior Capstone Project
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Understand the capstone project scope, milestones, and evaluation criteria
Form balanced teams based on complementary skill sets
Begin ideation using structured brainstorming techniques
readings: McConnell, Software Project Survival Guide, Ch. 1-3
Selected software project postmortems from IEEE Software

lecture_notes.introduction: The senior capstone is your opportunity to demonstrate everything you have learned in the CS program. Over 15 weeks, your team will take a software product from concept to deployment, producing a portfolio-quality project and a professional postmortem.
key_concepts: Milestone-driven development: proposal, architecture, alpha, beta, release
Team roles and skill assessment: identifying strengths and gaps
Scope management: fitting ambition to timeline and team capability
Risk identification and mitigation planning
Version control workflow and CI/CD setup from day one
lecture_notes.demonstration: Review three previous capstone projects: one that succeeded by scoping well, one that pivoted mid-project, and one that overscoped; analyze the lessons from each
setup_requirements: Git repository created for team on GitHub
Project management tool configured (Linear, Jira, or GitHub Projects)

review.value: The smallest version of your product that delivers real value to users; scope your MVP to be achievable in 8 weeks, leaving 7 weeks for iteration, polish, and the unexpected problems that always arise
Quality, scope, and schedule form a constraint triangle; the capstone fixes schedule (15 weeks), so scope must flex to maintain quality. Cut features, never cut quality

learning_objectives: Present a complete project proposal with technical architecture and user research
Receive and incorporate faculty and peer feedback on feasibility
Establish a production plan with identified risks and contingencies
readings: Sample project proposals from previous semesters (provided on LMS)
Fowler, 'Is Design Dead?' (architecture evolution in agile)

lecture_notes.introduction: The proposal is your project's foundation. A thorough proposal prevents costly direction changes by forcing clarity on the problem, solution, architecture, and feasibility before any code is written.
key_concepts: Problem statement: what user problem does this solve and for whom
Technical architecture: system components, technology stack, data flow
User research: evidence that the problem exists and the solution is wanted
Feasibility assessment: mapping required work against team skills and time
Risk register: what could go wrong and how you will respond
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents their proposal (10 minutes) followed by structured faculty and peer critique
setup_requirements: Completed proposal document uploaded to LMS

review.value: Document every significant technical decision with context, options considered, and rationale; these records prevent revisiting settled decisions and help new team members understand why the system is built the way it is

Choose technologies your team already knows for core components; the capstone is not the time to learn a new framework under deadline pressure. Reserve one experimental technology for a non-critical component if the team wants to learn something new

learning_objectives: Demonstrate a working prototype validating the core technical approach
Present the system architecture with component interactions
Show CI/CD pipeline and development workflow in action
readings: Bass, Software Architecture in Practice, Ch. 1-3
Continuous Delivery Pipeline documentation for your chosen stack

lecture_notes.introduction: The architecture milestone proves that your technical approach works. A working prototype answering the hardest technical question gives confidence that the remaining work is execution, not research.
key_concepts: Prototype scope: prove the riskiest technical assumption, nothing more
Architecture review: components, interfaces, data flow, deployment
CI/CD demonstration: automated build, test, deploy on every merge
Development workflow: branching strategy, code review, task tracking
Performance and security considerations in the architecture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team demonstrates their prototype and walks through their architecture, CI/CD pipeline, and development workflow

review.value: When facing technical uncertainty, time-box a spike (prototype) to answer the specific question, then throw away the spike code and implement properly. Spike code that goes to production is technical debt born on day one
Deploy your application to a staging environment in the first week; every subsequent milestone builds on a working deployment rather than scrambling to deploy at the end

learning_objectives: Deliver a feature-complete alpha release with all core functionality working
Present user testing results and feedback integration plan
Demonstrate automated test coverage and quality metrics
readings: Selected readings on user testing methodology

lecture_notes.introduction: Alpha means feature-complete: every planned core feature is implemented and functional, even if rough. After alpha, focus shifts to bug fixing, performance, polish, and user feedback integration. No new features after alpha.
key_concepts: Feature completeness: all user stories for MVP are implemented
User testing: at least 5 external users have tested the alpha
Bug tracking: systematic triage with severity and priority
Test coverage: automated tests cover critical paths
Feature freeze discipline: resisting scope creep after alpha
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents their alpha release with live demo, user testing results, bug list, and plan for beta
setup_requirements: User testing completed with documented results

review.value: After alpha, every request for a new feature is answered with 'file it for v2'; the remaining weeks are for quality, not scope expansion. Teams that add features after alpha invariably ship a buggy product
People who did not build the software will find problems the team is blind to within minutes; 5 external testers for 30 minutes each reveals more usability issues than weeks of internal testing

learning_objectives: Deliver a polished beta release with bugs fixed and performance optimized
Present load testing results and scalability analysis
Demonstrate security review findings and remediations
readings: Google SRE Book, Ch. 17-18 (on release engineering)
Performance testing best practices for your technology stack

lecture_notes.introduction: Beta means release-ready minus final polish. The beta should be stable, performant, and secure enough that you would be comfortable showing it to a potential employer. The final three weeks are for documentation, presentation prep, and last-mile polish.
key_concepts: Bug fix verification: every fixed bug has a regression test
Performance testing: load test results meet defined targets
Security review: OWASP checklist addressed
Documentation: API docs, user guide, deployment guide
Polish priorities: what to spend the final weeks on for maximum impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents their beta with performance data, security review results, and a plan for the final release

review.value: Every bug fix must include an automated test that would have caught the original bug; without this, the same bug will reappear when related code changes
Define acceptable response times before testing (e.g., API responses under 200ms at P95, page load under 2 seconds); test against these budgets, not just 'does it feel fast enough'

learning_objectives: Deliver a final release suitable for public use
Present a professional postmortem analyzing the development process
Reflect on team dynamics, technical decisions, and personal growth
readings: Selected classic postmortems from software engineering literature
Career preparation: presenting technical projects in interviews

lecture_notes.introduction: The final release is your finished product. The postmortem is your honest reflection on how you built it. Together, they represent the culmination of your CS education and become cornerstone portfolio pieces for your career.
key_concepts: Release checklist: no critical bugs, documentation complete, deployment automated
Postmortem format: what went right, what went wrong, lessons learned
Honest self-assessment: acknowledging failures is more valuable than claiming perfection
Portfolio presentation: framing the project for employers
Individual contribution documentation for each team member
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents their final release (15-minute live demo) followed by their postmortem (10 minutes) with faculty Q&A

review.value: The most valuable postmortems are brutally honest about failures and what caused them; hiring managers value self-awareness and learning ability over claims of flawless execution
When presenting the capstone in interviews, clearly state your specific role and contributions, the hardest technical challenge you personally solved, and what you would do differently with more time

major_assignments.title: Milestone 1: Project Proposal
major_assignments.description: Produce a comprehensive project proposal defining the problem, solution, target users, technical architecture, production plan, and risk register.
objectives: Define a clear problem statement backed by user research evidence
Design a technical architecture appropriate for the problem and team skills
Produce a realistic production plan with milestones and risk mitigations
Demonstrate collaborative team planning with role assignments
deliverables: Project proposal (8-10 pages) with problem statement, user research, architecture, tech stack justification, timeline, and risk register
Architecture diagram showing all system components and their interactions
10-minute team presentation with Q&A
Team charter with roles, communication protocols, and conflict resolution

criteria: Problem is clearly defined with evidence of user need
Solution addresses the problem directly
Target users are identified with supporting research
Scope is appropriate for the team size and timeline
Architecture is well-designed with clear component separation
Technology choices are justified with specific reasoning
Data model and API design are included
Deployment strategy is defined
Timeline is realistic with appropriate buffer
Risk register identifies genuine risks with mitigation plans
Team roles leverage individual strengths
Communication and workflow protocols are established
Clear problem definition with evidence-based user need
Sound technical architecture with justified technology choices
Realistic production plan with comprehensive risk management
Strong team organization with clear roles and communication

major_assignments.title: Milestone 2: Architecture and Prototype
major_assignments.description: Deliver a working prototype that validates the core technical approach, a detailed architecture document, and a functioning CI/CD pipeline.
objectives: Prove the riskiest technical assumption through a working prototype
Document the system architecture with component diagrams and interface specifications
Establish a CI/CD pipeline that builds, tests, and deploys automatically
Demonstrate effective team collaboration through version control history
deliverables: Working prototype deployed to staging environment
Architecture document with component diagram, API specifications, and data model
CI/CD pipeline configuration with automated testing
Sprint retrospective report

criteria: Prototype demonstrates the core technical challenge is solvable
Prototype runs without critical bugs
Deployment is automated and repeatable
Code quality is acceptable for a foundation to build on
Architecture document is complete and accurate
Component interfaces are specified clearly
Data model supports all planned features
Security and performance considerations are addressed
CI/CD pipeline runs on every push with tests
Version control shows meaningful commits from all team members
Sprint retrospective identifies specific improvements
Task tracking shows organized workflow
Working prototype validating core technical approach
Comprehensive architecture documentation
Automated CI/CD pipeline with testing
Evidence of effective team collaboration

major_assignments.title: Milestone 3: Alpha Release
major_assignments.description: Deliver a feature-complete alpha release with all core user stories implemented, user testing results from at least 5 external testers, and comprehensive automated test coverage.
objectives: Achieve feature completeness for all MVP user stories
Conduct user testing with external participants
Establish comprehensive automated test coverage
Document and triage all known bugs
deliverables: Feature-complete alpha build deployed to staging
User testing report with data from at least 5 external testers
Automated test suite with coverage report
Bug tracker with all known issues categorized

criteria: All MVP user stories are implemented and functional
Features work as specified in the proposal
Edge cases are handled gracefully
No placeholder functionality in core features
At least 5 external testers with documented sessions
Quantitative and qualitative data collected
Findings are actionable and prioritized
Design changes are justified by testing data
Automated tests cover critical user paths
Test suite runs in CI on every push
Known bugs are tracked with severity ratings
Code quality is maintained across the codebase
Feature-complete alpha demonstrating all core functionality
Rigorous user testing with data-driven iterations
Comprehensive automated testing in CI pipeline
Systematic bug tracking with prioritized triage

major_assignments.title: Milestone 4: Beta Release
major_assignments.description: Deliver a polished beta release with bugs fixed, performance verified under load, security review completed, and user documentation written.
objectives: Fix all critical and major bugs from alpha
Verify performance under load meets defined targets
Complete a security review addressing OWASP concerns
Write user-facing documentation
deliverables: Polished beta build deployed to production environment
Load test results with performance analysis
Security review report with remediation status
User documentation and API documentation

criteria: All critical and major bugs from alpha are fixed
No new critical bugs in beta
Regression tests added for all fixed bugs
Application is stable under normal usage
Load test conducted with realistic scenarios
Performance meets defined targets
Bottlenecks identified and addressed
Results documented with methodology
OWASP checklist reviewed with findings addressed
Authentication and authorization are robust
User documentation covers all features
API documentation is complete and accurate
Stable beta with all critical bugs resolved
Performance verified under load with data
Security review completed with remediations
Complete user and API documentation

major_assignments.title: Milestone 5: Final Release and Postmortem
major_assignments.description: Deliver the final release suitable for public use, accompanied by a professional postmortem and public presentation.
objectives: Deliver a polished, deployable product meeting all quality standards
Write a professional postmortem analyzing the full development process
Present the project to faculty and industry guests
Document individual contributions and personal growth
deliverables: Final release deployed to production with automated deployment
Postmortem document (8-10 pages) analyzing what went right, what went wrong, and lessons learned
Public presentation (25 minutes: 15-minute live demo + 10-minute postmortem)
Individual reflection essay (2-3 pages) on personal contributions and growth
Complete source code repository with documentation

criteria: No critical bugs in the release
Application is performant and secure
All features work as documented
Deployment is automated and repeatable
Product delivers real value to target users
Honest analysis of successes and failures
Root causes identified, not just symptoms
Lessons learned are specific and actionable
Writing is professional quality
Live demo runs smoothly
Postmortem presentation is engaging
All team members contribute meaningfully
Q&A responses demonstrate deep understanding
Honest assessment of personal contributions
Specific examples of challenges overcome
Connection to career development goals
Self-awareness about growth areas
Polished final release meeting professional quality standards
Insightful postmortem demonstrating genuine learning
Professional presentation to faculty and guests
Thoughtful individual reflection on growth and contributions

milestone_deliverables.description: Five milestone submissions tracking project progress

final_product.description: Quality and completeness of the final deployed product

postmortem.description: Written analysis of development process and lessons learned

peer_evaluation.description: Team contribution assessment from all team members

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all milestone reviews, user testing sessions, and the final presentation. Missing a milestone review without prior arrangement results in a zero for that milestone.
course_policies.late_submissions: Milestone deadlines are firm. Late submissions are penalized 15% per day. The final release deadline is absolute.
course_policies.collaboration: This is a required team project. All team members must contribute substantively. Peer evaluations adjust individual grades.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original team work or properly attributed open-source. Using code from other teams or previous semesters is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Teams must maintain a Git repository, CI/CD pipeline, project management tool, and communication platform. All releases must be deployed to an accessible environment.
